text: On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the 15th.
In the last review, your team told us that the installation was on track.
Your planner claimed that the cable had been dispatched.
Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days.
João, good morning. Before we start, I want to say that we are making real progress on site. The team is working weekends.

Thank you, Elena. Let me start with the number, and then I would like to hear you. The schedule variance on the installation is minus thirty working days. The activity is on the critical path, so the float is zero: nothing absorbs this.
We are aware of the delay. But you have to understand: the interface drawings arrived late from your side, and my team could not start.
To some extent, both parties share responsibility for the drawings, and I am not going to pretend otherwise. They were issued eight days late. The slippage we are discussing is thirty days.
And I never promised you the fifteenth. I said we would do our best. There is a difference, João.

There is. That is why I am reading from the minutes. On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth, and the minutes were circulated to you the same afternoon. On 12 June your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days. Twenty-two days have passed, and nothing has been received.
All right. That is fair. The schedule was not sent, and I should have called you. What do you need from us today?

Three things, and then I will let you work. A recovery schedule by Friday, showing how the thirty days are won back. A contingency plan if the retest fails, because I need one either way. And a weekly call, so that the next slip reaches me in three days and not in three weeks. If we can agree that today, we do not need the steering committee in April.
The schedule variance is minus thirty working days, and the activity is on the critical path.
On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth.
To some extent, both parties share responsibility. The float, however, belongs to the project.
If we can reach a consensus today, this does not have to go to the steering committee.

João, it is Elena. I know we speak tomorrow at ten, and that is exactly why I am calling you tonight. I would rather you heard this from me than in front of my director. The reason the installation is six weeks late is not the drawings. A subcontractor lost a cable batch in April, and I was told it had been dispatched. It had not. I found out three weeks later, and I did not escalate it, because I believed we could recover it quietly. We could not. So tomorrow, when you read out the minutes, you will be right. On the third of June I did tell you the relay would ship on the fifteenth, and I meant it when I said it. The recovery schedule was never sent, and I should have called you. What I do not accept, and I will say this tomorrow as well, is that the whole variance is ours. Your interface drawings were eight days late, and my team could not start without them. Eight days of that thirty are yours. Now, what I want to ask you, and you are completely free to say no. Please do not take this to the steering committee in April. If it goes there, my company will send a lawyer and not an engineer, and you and I will stop solving this. Give me until Friday. You will have a recovery schedule, a second crew at our cost, and a call every Monday morning for as long as you want one. And João, one more thing. You have every right to be angry tomorrow. I would be.

Before you chair that review, three rules, because engineers chair these meetings badly and it costs them years. First. A review meeting does not exist to establish who is guilty. It exists to establish what is true, what has changed, and who does what by when. Blame is a by-product; the date is the product. If you walk out of that room having won the argument and having agreed nothing, you have lost, and you will be back in the same room in six weeks. Second, and this is the expensive one. Never agree a new date before you know the cause. A supplier who is late will always offer you a new date, immediately, because the new date ends the uncomfortable conversation. But a date agreed without a cause is a guess dressed up as a commitment, and it will slip a second time, on your schedule and at your cost. Ask what happened. Ask for the plan that fixes what happened. Only then talk about dates. Third. The float belongs to the project, not to the supplier. They will ask you for it. They will ask nicely, and they will call it flexibility. But the moment you hand over two weeks of float so that somebody can cover their own delay, you have paid for their mistake with time that you will need, later, when something goes wrong that nobody planned for. And something always does. And one last thing, about how you sound. You do not need to be aggressive in that meeting. You need to be precise. Read the date, name the commitment, use the word they used, and then be quiet. Silence, after a fact, does more work than anger ever will.
On 3 June you assured us that the relay would ship on the fifteenth.
In the last review your team told us that the installation was on track.
Your planner claimed that the cable had already been dispatched.
Your company undertook to submit a recovery schedule within ten days.
The activity is on the critical path, so there is no float to absorb the delay.
If we can reach a consensus today, this does not have to go to the steering committee.
The schedule variance is minus thirty working days, and the activity is on the critical path.
To some extent, both parties share responsibility. The float, however, belongs to the project.
